title: SEGMENT 1 — Why this case matters — the central question of "can we build a medicine for one person?"

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — Why this case matters — the central question of "can we build a medicine for one person?"
Alright.
Let's begin.
Today, for the next two hours, we are going to walk through a single medical case.
One patient.
One drug.
One paper.

And by the end of those two hours, I want you to understand why this particular case — published in the New England Journal of Medicine in October of 2019, the lead authors Kim, Hu, and Moufawad El Achkar, working out of Boston Children's Hospital under Tim Yu — why this single case may turn out to be one of the most important medical papers of the early twenty-first century, even though it concerns a sample size of exactly one human being.
The patient's name was Mila Makovec.
She was six years old when she received her first dose of the drug that was made for her.
The drug was called milasen — m-i-l-a-s-e-n — and the name is exactly what it sounds like.
It is named after her.
It will never be given to anyone else.
It was designed, manufactured, tested in animals, cleared by the FDA, and infused into a child's spinal fluid in less than one calendar year.
And there has never, in the history of medicine, been a drug like that before.
I want to start with the question the paper implicitly asks, because if you carry one question with you through the whole lecture, this is the one.
Can we build a medicine for one person?
That sounds like a strange question.
Of course we build medicines.
Pharmacies are full of them.
But every drug you have ever taken — every aspirin, every antibiotic, every insulin shot, every chemotherapy infusion — was built for a population.

A drug company spent ten or fifteen years and a billion dollars or more developing it, ran it through three phases of clinical trials with thousands of subjects, got it approved by a regulator that demands evidence of efficacy across many patients, and then sold it to the millions of people who happen to share whatever disease it treats.
The economics, the ethics, the regulatory machinery, the manufacturing — all of it is designed for the many.
Not the one.
Now think about what that means for someone with a truly rare disease.

If you have a disease that only ten thousand people in the world share, the math of drug development still kind of works — barely — because ten thousand people times a high price per dose can still pay back the development cost.
But what if you have a disease that only a hundred people share?
Or ten?
Or — and this is Mila's case — exactly one?
Your specific mutation has been seen, in the entire history of medical literature, in you and in no one else.
The economic engine that built every drug on every pharmacy shelf does not work for you.
It cannot work for you.
There is no market.
There is no patient population.
There is no commercial drug company that will ever invest the money to make your medicine, because they will sell, at most, one dose to one patient and then never again.
For most of medical history, that meant such patients were simply out of luck.

We knew their diagnosis, we knew the molecular cause of their disease, we even sometimes had a theoretical idea of what kind of drug would fix it — and we couldn't do anything for them, because the system wasn't built to treat a single person.
The milasen paper is the first published proof that this is no longer necessarily true.

For a certain class of mutations — a certain class of molecular problems — we now have a technology platform flexible enough, fast enough, and cheap enough to design and deliver a brand-new medication for an individual patient.
Not in fifteen years.
In one.
Not for a billion dollars.
For a few million.
Not after thousands of clinical trial subjects.
After one.
And the paper we are studying today is the case report that proved it could be done.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do three things — one, sketch on a napkin what an antisense oligonucleotide is and explain in plain English how a splice-switching ASO works; two, walk through Mila's diagnostic odyssey and explain why standard genetic testing missed half of her disease and what kind of testing finally caught it; three, articulate honestly what the milasen treatment did for her, what it didn't do, and what kinds of patients in the future this approach can and
cannot help — then we have succeeded.
Everything else hangs off those three.
There is one more thing I want to say before we go further, and I want to say it now because it shapes the tone of everything we will discuss for the next two hours.
Mila Makovec died on February 18, 2021, at the age of ten.
The paper we are studying was published in October of 2019.

The drug we will spend most of this lecture admiring — its molecular cleverness, the speed of its development, the elegance of its mechanism — slowed her seizures and stabilized parts of her clinical picture, but it did not, in the end, save her life.
Her brain continued to atrophy.
Her disease continued to progress, more slowly than it would have without the drug, but it progressed.
And the day came when the slowing wasn't enough.
I tell you this now because I do not want you to leave this lecture with a feeling that medicine has solved the problem of rare disease.
It hasn't.
What this paper proved is that we have, for the first time, the technical capacity to try.
That is not the same as a cure.

It is, however, an extraordinary new beginning, and the rest of this lecture is the story of how that beginning happened, what it required, and what it teaches us about the future of medicine for the rarest patients on Earth.
